reasoning: Russia has not conducted a nuclear test since 1990, adhering to a well-established moratorium. While recent years have seen increased nuclear rhetoric, a revoking of the CTBT ratification (to mirror the US stance), and maintained readiness at the Novaya Zemlya test site, Russian leadership has explicitly stated it will only resume nuclear testing if the United States does so first. Given that the US is highly unlikely to test, and considering the significant geopolitical backlash a Russian test would trigger—particularly alienating key partners like China and nations in the Global South—the probability of a nuclear test occurring remains very low. The baseline rate over the last 34 years is 0, and the strategic deterrence value of an actual test does not outweigh the diplomatic costs under current conditions.
